students.name: Abdullah Bin Masood
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
Two things really stuck with me from our discussions and they both kind of connect in a deeper way than it first seems

First was the whole idea of stepping beyond the technical layer and asking questions about free will consciousness and what it actually means to be human in a world that is becoming increasingly shaped by technology. Usually when we talk about tech we stay at the level of systems efficiency algorithms scaling performance. But the moment you bring in free will and consciousness you are forced to confront limits. Not just limits of machines but limits of understanding itself

If consciousness cannot be reduced to computation then no matter how advanced systems get there is still a gap. If it can be reduced then it raises uncomfortable questions about identity and agency. Either way it forces you out of autopilot thinking. It also explains why the discussion kept jumping between religion philosophy and technology. Those are not separate domains here they are different attempts at answering the same core question from different angles. 

The second thing that stood out was the selfish gene idea. The claim is not just that people act selfishly in obvious ways but that even acts that look selfless might still be rooted in self preservation or internal reward structures. Thing like killing yourself to save your lover or being a kamikaze pilot. That can feel cynical at first but I do not think it has to be. If anything it strips away the illusion that humans are purely noble or purely rational and replaces it with something more grounded

What makes it interesting is how it changes interpretation rather than behavior. You start to see actions differently. Helping someone might still be genuinely meaningful even if part of it satisfies your own sense of identity or moral alignment. Ambition might be tied to validation but that does not automatically make it shallow.

Before this week I had a pretty narrow and honestly human-centered definition of intelligence. I associated it with things like reasoning, problem solving etc. Basically the stuff we reward in school or see in top performers in technical fields. Even when I thought about AI, I was comparing it to that same benchmark. Can it reason like us, can it solve problems like us, can it replicate human cognition

What shifted for me was realizing that this definition is more of a bias than a truth. The caterpillar example kind of cracked that open in a subtle but powerful way. A caterpillar cutting through a leaf to escape a predator does not look intelligent in the traditional sense. There is no visible reasoning, no language, no conscious planning in the way we would describe it. But at the same time, the behavior is adaptive and effective. It is solving a survival problem using mechanisms that evolved over time.

The question that stuck with me most is around consciousness and whether AI could ever reach something comparable to human-level experience. Not just performance or intelligence, but actual awareness. That question feels way deeper than it first sounds, because it forces you to separate things we usually bundle together

One version of the question I now have is what even counts as consciousness. Is it self-awareness, the ability to reflect on your own thoughts, or something more like subjective experience where there is a felt sense of being alive. Because if it is just self-reflection, then you could imagine machines getting there eventually. But if it is subjective experience, then it becomes much harder to even define, let alone replicate.
Sure but anonymously.

students.name: Adeshvir Dhanoa
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
This week's class dealt with difficult questions humans tried answering for centuries:

What is intelligence? What is free will? Do we possess free will? What is human nature? What is selfish? What is selfishness? Who gave us these attributes? Why were we given these attributes? Is there a defined path already written for us?

As we progress in technology and create systems trying to mimic human-like intelligence, we come across these questions that define us as a human species. It would be in our favor to look at ancient wisdom and frameworks that gave birth to where we stand today as a society.

The particular topic that stood out to me this week was the discussion of how all humans, beings, animals are innately "selfish". Meaning, they are trying to maximize a choice, (a value function) for their own physical, emotional, mental, or spiritual gain.

Being from a Sikh background, I use my fundamental religious identity and ideology to help guide me through these topics and questions. While this being said my religion does also promote learning about what other religions state, as wisdom is everywhere and eternal. 

So here's my take on it:

I whole-heartedly disagree with the statement of "every human being 'selfish' " but can't explain why. I tried asking Claude to guide me on what the world's major religions said about intelligence, human nature, selfishness, and free will with scripture lines for evidence and guidance. It even gave me reasoning behind these ideologies, and despite having a full blown conversation with Claude on this topic, I am still confused on what to say. But for now, I have come up with this. The soul, per Dharmic belief, is a part of God, and intelligence is an aspect of the soul which gives us the ability to reason, discern, and understand, but to be clear these are abilities of having intelligence not intelligence itself. Because I can throw a basketball into a net doesn't define me as a basketball player, but me not being on a NBA team doesn't define that I am not a basketball player either. So by my own definition, intelligence is intelligence, it is something that can't be explained, the same way God, the Universal Truth can't be explained, it just 'is'.

This intelligence 'gifted' to humans per Abrahamic and Dharmic faiths helps us discern between right and wrong; helps us judge our own actions; and gives us the capability to override the value function. It gives us the capability to do things just to do it. Not because we were told to, not because we are relying on a reward, but for the mere fact of doing the action to simply do it. In the Bhavagad Gita, Arjuna was told by Krishna to act upon his duties for the sake of it, not for a reward.

All major world religions remind us to stop acting out of ego, and to recognize God. And while many religions claim the fruition behind recognizing God and doing good deeds, isn't that in itself maximizing a value function? If we recognize God and do good deeds just to do it, have we stopped the cycle of the value function? If I stop acting out of ego, lust, glutton, anger, and pride, and just act in accordance to how God wants me to act, aren't I still trying to maximize a value function? But if I cut out the fact I am trying to maximize to act in accordance to God, and just doing it to do it without the thought of a reward or the thought of I have to do it or I should do it, am I still deemed "selfish"?

While there is a huge difference acting out of ego and sin, and for good and God, doesn't it mean we are still fundamentally trying maximize a value function?

This is still without digging into free will, and if we are in a "pre-destined path". Are there infinite realties and paths already known by God based on our actions? 

What I am trying to say is that, AI bots have a motive, been designed, to act in a certain way to maximize something. Humans in hind sight have been designed in this way as well, but through intelligence I believe humans can override this which makes us different from AI.

I believe my standpoint of AI definitely has been challenged this week. The human species has claimed to be different from every other thing alive, even within in its own species, to be proposed as unique and different. The mere fact, the something we created has the capability to be like us or even surpass us is an interesting yet terrifying thought. If we are all trying to maximize value functions, what makes us truly different from AI. What separates humanity from AI?

What separates human intellect from AI intellect? 

Is there a dissolution of human intelligence due to AI?

Is it merging to become one?
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Andnet DeBoer
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
From our discussion I think it became immediately obvious we needed to lay the groundwork by first defining what we mean by intelligence. Furthermore, I lay out some additional thoughts on topics we touched on that I found particularly interesting or thought provoking with my take.

 

My Sense-Think-Act Framework

From my background in robotics I initially considered 3 core pillars to be the foundations of an intelligent agent: Sense, Think, Act. Each intelligent thing needs some sort of way to perceive its environment, process or compute upon data from that environment, and then act or apply some change back to it. This definition can be utilized to describe intelligent things at their macro or nano scale. For non-biological systems like machines this definition also still applies, albeit more loosely. A neural network, for example, has an input, an internal process, and an output.

This seems reasonable at first glance but it has a problem. This description is far too general, describing more of a process than intelligence specifically. By this approach a function in programming would have fallen under this definition since it takes input, applies a computation, then produces an output. So that begs the question: what are the actual requirements for the computation step in order to qualify it as intelligence? 

Learning is the Differentiator

In class we discussed aspects of the computation step that are essential for intelligence, and the primary argument was that an intelligent agent must be able to learn. This means the agent must be able to update and or upgrade its own computation step with new information, patterns, or objectives that arise. This implies an agent which is learning over time should be able to have a measurable increase in its cognitive fitness for its environment over a given time interval after the learning update.By this I mean that the computation step generally improves with each update, not degrades.

After some consideration I believe this is one of the key distinctions that separates intelligence from a general process. A basic function doesn't revise itself. Furthermore, this also touches on another key dependency of learning, memory. 

In order to maintain updates to its cognition or computational step an intelligent agent needs to preserve what it has learned in the past. It must therefore have memory to store this information. 

What is an agent?

Now I would like to define an agent more precisely. An agent has intelligence as described above but it also has a domain (essentially its embodiment). For a human that's our body's anatomy, for a robot that's its form factor, and for AI that's its compute servers. I believe that intelligence is grounded in and constrained by the physical or computational substrate it inhabits. 

To exist is to have some defined unit,  a sort of agentic boundary. In the metaphysical sense this is the essence of identity. We have a certain number of molecular components that make up our human embodiment and that is the territory or domain upon which our minds have agency. We are simply the sum of what we have direct agency over.

 

Review of Intelligence

Ok, so through this prolonged thought experiment a few aspects of intelligence have emerged for me. 

Intelligence requires the following:

* Observation- An ability to take in some information

* Process - An ability to process the information perform some analysis or computation

* Learn - To learn or improve the computation/analysis process and store it with Memory

* Output - Produce an output

 

Therefore, I must conclude that modern AI does indeed meet the core ingredients for these emergent requirements for intelligence even though this doesn’t sit right with me.

How to measure intelligence?

After sitting on this sort of unpleasant conclusion it became clear it was time to see if there were any ways to reason one would go about quantifying intelligence. 

As we discussed in class many people, especially those who received western or American education often associate Albert Einstein with intelligence. Even though they have never read one of his papers. How could this become so? How do we determine intelligence? How would we go about measuring intelligence? 

The problem is this is a multi-dimensional and only partially observable metaphysical process which is complex and entangled. Similar to our difficulty in interpreting the black box of modern LLM’s and large foundation models, it is even more complex to disentangle the workings of the mind. Intelligence can be exhibited in many domains from physical, mental, emotional, to any available modality found in our universe. As such the way it is manifested and measured by one agent in one domain and in one environment at a certain time step does not necessarily give any notable insight into the intelligence of another agent or group of agents.

My perspective on whether AI is truly intelligent or simply an emulator of intelligence has gotten significantly more fuzzy since the first class.

Prior to this discussion I would have been firmly in the camp that AI is essentially a very complex mirage.  I saw it as something capable of fooling us into thinking it's intelligent without actually being so. I came in pretty skeptical. As a computer scientist was never a proponent of the Turing test, which essentially puts human belief as a proxy for intelligence. I would argue tests like this are purely external and behavioral, measuring the appearance of intelligence from the outside with no regard for what is actually happening inside the system. It pretty much conflates the performance of intelligence with intelligence itself. And given how exceedingly easy it is to fool humans, the fact that a system can pass these 'intelligence benchmarks' tells us more about our own perceptual limitations and evaluations than it does about the system being evaluated.

Through reviewing the class and thinking about my own perspective on the prerequisites for intelligence I found myself building a bar that modern AI systems increasingly clear. The framework I constructed to distinguish intelligence from a general process didn't really exclude AI. It started to sound more like a description of AI itself. And that conclusion, as I noted, doesn't sit right with me, but I'm having a hard time arguing my way out of it.

What changed most isn't my answer, it's my confidence in my answer. I came in thinking the question of AI intelligence was essentially pretty obvious, AI is not intelligent. Now, I'm leaving this week thinking the question is more open ended than I thought. Either my definition is still incomplete, or my new intuition is wrong, or both. 

The main dimension I think my original framework is still missing is sentience. The question of whether there is something that understands its own existence. A sufficiently sophisticated process can learn, remember, update, and output. But that doesn't tell us if its sentient. And until I can articulate that requirement for sentience more precisely, I can't fully distinguish "meets the criteria" and "actually intelligence".

What does it mean for an agent to be sentient? 

I want to look at existing philosophy on this topic to see if there are any succinct frameworks to define this phenomena. 

What are the dimensions of intelligence? 

I want to think through more closely the aspects of intelligence from very commonly observed and discussed to more rare manifestations of intelligence
Sure but anonymously.

students.name: Andre Shportko
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers: I found it interesting the a question of free will was brought up as a criteria for intelligence. It implies that entities that possess free will are intelligent; however, this would imply that machines are automatically disqualified from being intelligent since the algorithms are deterministic (even if some processes are (pseudo)-probabilistic). Since I believe that artificial intelligence exists, by contrapositive, it gives me a hint that free will might be neither necessary, not sufficient for intelligence.

The caterpillar example sparked a discussion in a room which relates the ancient debate of nature v. nurture. Just because smart survival strategies have been inherited as opposed to acquired, does it imply that this animal is not intelligent? On this issue, I partially side with prof. Alam: it seems to me that human intelligence is also a result of evolution and natural selection and that is there is little meaningful differences between acquired and innate intelligence that would separate humans from rest of the animal kingdom.

Professor Alam observed that humans have a anthropocentric bias about what does it mean to evaluate intelligence. As an example, he noticed that LLMs have unusual error profile -- they might be unable to count number of letters in a word , but they might be able to solve complex physics task. For humans, this is very unusual: if someone is licensed to launch rockets, we expect them to be also to be able to count number of letters. 

 

I would explore error profiling by demonstrating that tasks complexity vs performance is more independent for AI than for humans (where skill acquisition is sequential in terms of complexity).
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Casey Zhang
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers: What stood out me the most is the three questions the professor asked in class, more like an experiment, regarding the first thing on mind when thinking of genius. I, like most of other students in class, thought about Einstein. I am very surprised that most of the people, including me, had Einstein in my mind. That's absolutely shocking to me. But I really can't think of anyone else's name. I also realized how much I am impacted by the society's education, including my definition regarding genius, how I perceived genius or Einstein. It's totally shaped by the environment, what I learnt from maybe elementary school. That is also to say, I do not have my own structured definition or reasoning of "genius". The following discussion over this topic is also interesting. We were asked why we thought of him under the fact that nobody ever met him and few people read his books. Basically, we didn't know him but we considered him as a genius rather someone else in the real life. I recognized the reason why I thought about Einstein is because everyone thinks he is a genius. It's like the universal fact though it should not be. This discussion reminds me how opinions can be easily shaped and I need to change my way of thinking and reasoning. Apparently, I need to be more careful and there is the need to construct my own system of processing information, what I knew (are they facts or opinions), what I should believe and based on what information supports the conclusion I got.

The question regarding if ChatGPT is intelligent. Well, my initial answer is absolutely yes. We all talk about artificial intelligence, and of course llm is under the field of AI. However, as we discuss the definition of intelligence, I started to question. I guess now I considered intelligence as a combination of biological intuition and learning. But apparently a pretrained model has limited ability to learn. What if intelligent is not an adjective used to describe an object such as human, animal or robot but instead an event or a specific action? I was thinking about the question if Einstein stops learning and only keeps the knowledge he possessed, is he still intelligent? From human's perspective, the purpose of owning intelligence is to survive in this world (in my opinion). When we think about caterpillar or ants, we see their capability to survive as something intelligent. Specific actions are also considered intelligent. Robots that deliver food or do surgeries are intelligent. But it's different from human intelligence. Then I guess there should be two types of intelligence, one is task-specific intelligence and the other is general intelligence. Robots don't need to survive in this world. They are not alive. They don’t understand what they’re doing in a human sense. Nor can they adapt to the environment outside their training. The tasks look intelligent, but the metals themselves may be not.

Are we treating AI as humans? Or are we designing AI with the goal to let it be as close as a human? Can we finally design an algorithm to let AI stand out of rules.

In general, topics regarding RULES are attracting to me. Humans can live within the rules, or against the rules. But it's difficult to discard the rules: stand entirely out of the rules (as an observer). AI works based on its knowledge base, which is composed of various types of rules. It's hard for humans to switch perspectives because humans are born with identity and personalities. We carry these within ourselves and usually will not attribute actions and thoughts to these factors (unconsciously). However, AI can switch personalities easily if we just told chatgpt to act like two different people, it can do so. Anyway, I am just curious about the possibility for ai to discard rules in its knowledge base, though that sounds impossible since that's not the way people design it.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Chenwan Zhong
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
1. The topic of how to define "intelligence": I believe that "the capacity to learn" is a highly legitimate criterion for defining intelligence. However, to distinguish human intelligence from AI, I would emphasize creativity. AI excels in execution—taking a well-developed concept and resolving technical hurdles or providing structured instructions. Yet, when it comes to generating truly original proposals or "blue-sky" ideas, human intuition remains irreplaceable. Also, in the discussion of why people think Albert Einstein is an undoubted genius, I'd also like to add that I think even when Einstein was at the end of his years, I would still appreciate the opportunity to have a talk with him, not for answers but for inspiration. I value people for the way they can think, and the perspectives they can bring cannot be replicated.

2. The theory that humans are "neurobiologically selfish"—in that all decisions are driven by internal neuronal mechanics—is fascinating. I totally agree with this theory, but it's the first time that I actually think of how neurons are "making decisions" inside our brain. Realizing that our choices are essentially the product of neurons "making decisions" has inspired me to bridge the gap between psychology and biology, leading me to explore the following research:

[1] Integrating memories to guide decisions [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cobeha.2015.08.010 ] (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cobeha.2015.08.010)

This paper fundamentally shifted my perspective on decision-making by framing it as a dynamic reconstruction rather than a simple reaction. It reveals that the hippocampus is not just a "filing cabinet" for the past, but a "simulator" for the future. I find it fascinating that our brain retrieves memories to run "mental rehearsals" before we commit to a choice. It aligns with my view on intelligence: true wisdom lies in the ability to creatively synthesize past experiences to navigate an unpredictable future.

[2] Dynamics of neural population responses in prefrontal cortex indicate changes of mind on single trials https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2014.05.049

What struck me most about this study is the visualization of a "change of mind" at the cellular level. It proves that a decision is not a single, static event, but a fluid neural trajectory that can pivot even at the last millisecond. Seeing the physical evidence of hesitation and revision within the prefrontal cortex makes me rethink the concept of "free will." It suggests that our final choices are the result of a competitive race between neural populations, which adds a profound layer of biological complexity to why we do what we do.

Just as these papers describe how neurons integrate memory and dynamic signals to create unique perspectives, I believe Einstein's genius was a result of a highly unique 'neural trajectory' that prioritized inspiration over mere calculation—a process that AI, for all its data-processing power, has yet to replicate.

Entering this week’s discussions, I didn't hold a rigid, predefined stance on AI or intelligence in general. Instead, I view my current state as a "formative phase"—I am in the process of dialectically listening to diverse perspectives and absorbing those that resonate with logic and evidence.

While my core beliefs haven't undergone a radical shift, the granularity of my understanding has deepened significantly. Before this week, my concept of AI was primarily functional—a tool for optimization. Now, through the lens of neuroscience, I see AI not just as a technical artifact, but as a mirror to our own biological constraints. It reinforced my belief that while AI can mimic the results of learning, the process of human creativity—rooted in the messy, dynamic trajectories of neural populations—remains a unique baseline for "true" intelligence. In short, this week didn't change my direction, but it provided a much clearer topographic map for the intellectual landscape I am navigating.

Question 1: How do neurons actually function inside our brain when we try to make a decision? (How can neuroscience explain consciousness and subconsciousness and tell them apart?)

I'd like to read more papers or talk to friends in the relevant fields to form a better opinion regarding this and prepare questions for our corresponding guest lecture.

Question 2: If inside a swarm, every individual can't explain what their motions or behaviours actually mean and completely doesn't understand how their behaviours can contribute to the swarm intelligence, can we still call them "intelligence"? Who should take credit for this intelligence?

I think the theory of Collective Intelligence helps when I try to answer this question myself. I'm going to look more into this theory and other related theories or philosophies.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Chenyu Zhu
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
When we are asked to vote if an ant is intelligent and if an ant colony is intelligent, more people voted for the colony. I also only raise up my hand for ant colony, likely due to the existence of ant colony optimization algorithm.

After that, I started to think why a group of ants is more clever than a single ant, while we as humans, always complain a group of people is more stupid than individuals. Then my guess is we as human beings are not individuals, but a group of neurons, so it makes sense that a group of neurons is more intelligent than a single neuron.

But why this logic doesn't work for a group of people? My guess is an ant, a neuron, is so unintelligent that they can only process the easiest information and do the simplest job. They are like single cores in a GPU. The communication efficiency between are very high so that they can be very powerful as a whole. But for humans, the communication(information exchange) is incredibly low, especially as a huge group.

 

For another topic, you said every one of us is optimizing a function when making decisions. I can't agree more. Actually, the first time I thought about this question was in my high school.

I didn't dive deep into why we are doing this in our mind all the time, nor I tried to persuade other people with my opinion. What I was thinking was, how to find the best optimization function so that when I unconsciously optimize the function, my decisions and actions will take me to a better future state. The optimization I found at that time was to keep my emotional fraction out of this function, because all the decisions dominated by emotions make me feel better at that time, but the reward fades away over time, so when I look back a few years later, that decision won't seem sensible.

Interestingly, as I gained age over time, I started to realize I should cherish my youth, and my current emotion plays a more and more important part when I get older. Therefore, my most recent version of optimization function added my emotional fraction back.

Before this class, I used to think AI is different with human. Though both of them are intelligent, they are fundamentally different. Humans have emotions and can do behaviors that AI can never do(free will mentioned in class), while AI can only do reasoning.

After the first class, I don't think the gap between human and AI is as huge as I expect. Though we are built differently, AI can do things that human can do, as long as given enough input and information. Our illusion that AI doesn't have free will is because AI are limited to only respond to human, and its sensor is not as sophisticated and its input data is not as comprehensive as humans. If AI also have touch sensor or even temperature and pressure sensor, instead of only vision and audio input, and if they are allowed to derive even when no one ask them, they can behave very similarly to human.

After this class, I think the gap between human and AI is not as huge as I expected(mentioned in Q2). So what makes us humans different from AI? Or are we actually more intelligent than AI? Is AI one day able to manage free will, creativity, emotion, etc. that we are proud of as a human?

I will interact with AI, make it generate text or images to describe something that doesn't exist in the world, and then see if it is a chimera of already existing things, or actually something new.

I will also ask AI to say something completely random or give weird suggestions, and then I will ask it to explain why they say this. If they can give a reason, then it is not completely random.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Daniel Wong
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
The topic that stood out to me the most during this week's discussion was our talk on the scenario of two lovers with a train about to come in relation to selfishness. I am honestly surprised by the viewpoint that our class took on it thinking about the scenario, as I believe that what our class thought only about the scenario from the viewpoint of what the individual gained instead of considering also about what they had lost. We discussed about how in the scenario of the boy choosing to throw himself in front of the train and letting himself die, that he is selfish because he gains no having to feel the grief of loosing his partner, but I believe that we should also consider what he has lost too, which is his life. students.name: Eric Fei
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
Two main topics stood out to me from the discussion we had on Friday: what constitutes an intelligent being and whether or not humans intrinsically selfish.

Regarding what constitutes intelligence, I think it was a very interesting discussion because it definitely probed at my fundamental understanding of what intelligence is. What I mean by this is that I had to think hard about what I thought an intelligent being was, and why I thought the way I did. My first thought, which I believe was shared by many of my classmates, was that an intelligent being is something that is "capable" in some way, for example solving a difficult math problem or performing some complicated task, like the caterpillar in the video that we saw. Upon further reflection I don't think this holds up, specifically for the reason that I shared in class: if being "capable" is the qualifying feature for intelligence, then we would need to declare things like automated assembly lines or complex mathematical equations like the Einstein Field Equation intelligent; certainly these things perform complex tasks, but by conventional wisdom we would not call them "intelligent" in the same way we would for a human or a caterpillar. This means that we either need to accept that a definition of intelligence as "capable" would encompass things like assembly lines, or we need to be more rigorous/particular about how we distinguish "intelligent" from "capable". I think the capacity for autonomous learning is the distinguishing factor, because it is what I think most cleanly distinguishes a "being" from a "process" like a machine or equation. One thing that was brought up in class was the idea that free will is the important factor that makes something intelligent; I think this is a very interesting point, but it needs to be predicated on the fact that free will exists in the first place, which is a very interesting discussion that currently seems very difficult to answer.

The second topic of discussion we had regarding whether or not humans are intrinsically selfish was also very interesting. I found it a fascinating assertion that everything we do as humans is an attempt by the brain to optimize a value function where our happiness is said value, with the example of a person pushing another person out of the way from a train and dying in the process being used to illustrate this point. I think it's definitely possible to argue humans are selfish, but at least for the specific example it requires that we empirically prove that the act of saving the person from the train and getting killed in the process actually makes the brain happier than the other option in order to show that choosing to save the person is a selfish act.

I think this week's conversation around intelligence has slightly changed my understanding about AI and whether or not it is truly intelligence. Like I asserted in my response to the previous question, I think the capacity for autonomous learning is what truly constitutes intelligence, and in this regard I think that certain AI systems certainly exhibit this ability and can be called intelligent. Therefore I think my thoughts haven't changed significantly since the "write down your thoughts" we had before class started, but they have become more specific.

Following from the hypothetical discussion we had about whether or not Einstein would still be considered intelligent if he lost the ability to learn, I think that not all AI systems can be called intelligent. For example, I would consider an LLM that has finished training and fine-tuning and has had all of its weights frozen as no longer intelligent but still capable. Because it has essentially lost the ability to increase its own knowledge or learn anything more, I think the LLM is no longer intelligent, and has essentially turned into a very capable "process" that can produce accurate/good answers given an input. However, if the LLM can continue to learn, then I would consider it intelligent as it has the ability to dynamically increases its knowledge.

I'm very curious about the academic and philosophical research that has gone into determining what intelligence is. There definitely seems to be a somewhat "common sense" consensus about intelligence is in our every day lives, but it doesn't seem to always hold up to closer examination, or when applied to certain edge cases like in our class discussion. I think an important aspect of having a debate is to clearly outline and agree about the definitions of the topics being discussed, so in this regard I think having a consensus on how the define intelligence would be very helpful. On this topic I plan to look for some research on intelligence, and see if there is any kind of scientific or academic consensus on what intelligence is.

In a similar vein, I have had a renewed interest in the topic of free will from the discussion we had in class. I think it's very interesting to think about whether or not free will exists, and it can lead into all kinds of fascinating discussions about topics like spirituality, is determinism true, does a potential lack of determinism actually imply the existence of free will, etc. If it is possible to prove that free will exists, and that things like humans possess it while for example a calculator does not, then I think free will as a quality could be very important to determining whether or not something can be considered intelligent. To look deeper into this I plan on doing some research online and seeing what discussions there are surrounding the existence of free will and what conclusions they have reached.
Umm...I don't know. Maybe ask me before posting my writing somewhere?

students.name: Ethan Foong
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
I think the "what is intelligence?" question, similar to the "do we have free will?" questions were both good and bad. Good in the sense that I think they are quite philisophically interesting questions that we probably can't good answers to without maybe some higher power or some way to truly probe our own or an animal's brains. But I also think they are relatively uninteresting to me because they don't give great direction into what to do next. 

Maybe I'm nitpicking here, but to me, the question is not what is intelligence and what is not, but a better question seems to be. Let's say that we draw the line of intelligence between humans and other animals or between animals and plants etc, then the obvious question is what creates the line. How is the system in an animal's brain any different than a human's. Or maybe more researchy, what measures could we use to determine the difference in ability that we see between regular animals and humans - to explain the difference in "prosperity" or "agency". To me, this seems like a more definable and more interesting question because it obviously has interesting implications for current AI, but also because its more concrete. I think if we really broke it down, a lot of our arguments for "what is intelligence" breaks down into language or just where we all personally draw the line. Similar to asking "if we assume that ChatGPT 4.5 is intelligence, then where do we draw the line between its older models to where we say that model is not intelligence." I'm sure you could go through and justify a line anywhere between any of those models and then go find the architecture or training that justifies your thinking, but in the end, that's just semantics and what's more interesting is to say, if we draw the line at every possible point there (or every major point) what are the architectural improvements that were made to make it more intelligent.

 

But it wasn't an unenjoyable question. But I think reflecting on it, it doesn't make too much sense to get into a debate that seems philisophical, but really is us just debating our personal lines on where intelligence is. I think its more interesting to get everyone's personal line and really drill into what is a measurable way to identify that line. For example, how can we get an AI to not just synthesize ideas it has memorized (which is a form of intelligence), but get to the point where it can do what Einstein does in his jump from classical mechanics to relativity. And maybe we'll find that all Einstein did is synthesize ideas he had memorized, but maybe also he did it in such a targeted way that he can make this big leap. Its hard to tell though, because I think with a lot of science, maybe if Einstein wasn't there, someone else would have made the leap, just 2-5 years in the future - in which case it would be the society itself that's intelligent and evolving its thinking not just him.

I think a similar argument can hold with "do we have free will." Maybe better to argue, if free will exists, how would we prove it than to take the question at just its face value.

I think maybe 2 things. One is I'm starting to believe more and more that belief is core to intelligence. That no matter how smart Einstein is, if he doesn't have core beliefs about what to investigate or maybe that there is something fundamentally wrong about Newtonian mechanics or he doesn't have the commitment to run thought experiments all day, then there is no way he can come up with his theories. That if you don't believe anything about the world or what's interesting or what you should pursue then you always get stuck between multiple domains or you always are trying to draw a conclusion when there doesn't necessarily have to be one. Maybe this is something our current AI is missing. 

Second thing is that I'm starting to believe more that intelligence is just goal-directed complexity. Human society has intelligence. I believe that even if you sat down one person with infinite time, they might not come up with all of what humanity has come up with so far. Because maybe that person has no motivation to come up with many of the social things we do. Why create the internet when there's no other people to communicate with. The second thing is that it feels to me that with just one perspective you do not have sufficient curiosity on a societal level to come up with all the things we do. For example, it might be right to say here that without art we never come up with modern technologies because all our inventors are not sufficiently inspired to invent. Without people who want to conquer or get rich we have no war and with no war comes a loss of many other technologies. Human society finds a way to invent these things not in spite of our split focus but because of it. Because we have many people willing to take certain subjects so far that they can come up with something new. I think AI can also do this, but only if they're willing to almost be multi personalitied and have each personality take intellectual risks. 

I also think maybe this analogy has interesting implications. We can maybe start to look at our own society for ways to make our AI better. Economists or finance companies can be looked at as maybe some form of attention, but one that has quite short term thinking or supports individual ideas (companies) more than the health of the society as a whole. A government can also direct attention, but it (is supposed to) promote societal health as a whole. I'm curious if we see this type of behavior switching or split in current models.

I think nothing too different than before. I'm quite interested currently on world modeling, but also motivation modeling. I think maybe part of the reason I think intelligence is partly based on your ability to create something "more new" than others is because I think current AI can't do this to a sufficient degree, but also because I think the potential solution here is to give AI some intrinsic motivation. Maybe its to discover all possible useful skills that it can perform with a robot arm. 

I think the "more new" part is interesting here because I don't think we see any AI today that can really learn from themselves or continue taking ideas or skills much further than their training data, but I want to see AI or robots talk to themselves or play with their own abilities to gain an understanding of the world and what they can do. A baby knows inherently when it hits a drum for the first time that it is an interesting phenomenon and it can target its investigation of that drum quite explicitly. It feels to me that current robots and AI cannot do this and I think this is where I'd like to investigate.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Florian Jule
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
I enjoyed questioning the definition of intelligence and free will. What is intelligent and do we have free will.

I like to think of an intelligent being as one that is capable to learn and adapt. Facing a new situation or given new data, a being can make decisions that maximizes some function. Most living creatures can seem intelligent, but I think evolution is to be thought about in a different way than intelligence itself. Those creatures can perform tasks that took millions of year to perfect, but are not capable of adaptation by learning a different way. Climate change is an example that might eradicate a number of species not capable of adapting, mostly because in one way or another, resources will lack and this will collapse a whole chain of living. Humans have the "chance" of being able to go wherever they desire on earth and beyond, by natural gift or by using technology, and are capable of surviving using (probably) the widest range of food available or fabricate some.

Thinking about free will in the context of intelligence and AI brings it down to: are we more than machines processing inputs and deterministically providing an output? I fundamentally do not think so. From the day we are born, our brain automatically adjusts the weights of its neural network based on the experiences we face and this drives our thoughts and all else follows. I still like to think of free will as the tendency to make some small high level decisions, for all else I believe we are passengers processing our environment. I believe that thinking this way allows me to be content with most of what I do (good and bad). If I accept that I am 100% a passenger, my life would likely (or not!) become very uninteresting. By believing that I can make small decisions about what I want to do, while accepting my thoughts are mostly a process of my environment, I find that I can purposefully put myself in situations of struggle (intellectually or with sports), which can lead to a sentiment of deep satisfaction, which to me summarizes the essence of human life (we are here to struggle).

In the end adaptation and learning can be thought of as an "advanced" form of evolution that allows the gene to thrive and survive. By that definition nothing is intelligent, only executing a script (from our DNA). All beings are survival machines with no free will and we only have one purpose.

 

 

Another subject we touched on was selfishness and who do we love most. The book "the selfish gene" by Richard Dawkins touches on this (extract from wikipedia):
Dawkins discusses kin selection: "Close relatives – kin – have a greater than average chance of sharing genes. It has long been clear that this must be why altruism by parents toward their young is so common. What R. A. Fisher, J. B. S. Haldane and especially W. D. Hamilton realized was that the same applies to other close relations—brothers and sisters, nephews and nieces, close cousins. If an individual dies in order to save ten close relatives, one copy of the kin-altruism gene may be lost, but a larger number of copies of the gene is saved.”

Over the last few years I have changed my view about free will and intelligence, mostly based on reading and discussing these topics with friends. I could see myself when hearing some of my fellow classmates answer questions on those topics which is always fascinating. 

From simple beings such as a caterpillar, to large language model, I find it really hard to pin down what intelligence means, and ultimately what are thoughts. Is intelligence more than processing data? This just feels like a unsatisfactory answer.

In our world, intelligence is relative. We say someone is intelligent if they are more intelligent that the majority of the people we know, we say they are a genius if they are within the 1% brightest people we know. This makes AI and robotics scary to think of, because it is already probably brighter than 99% of human beings from a reasoning (sadly humans are not rational) point of view and 100% from a knowledge point of view. Robotics is the missing piece that will bring physical capabilities to the machines. Maybe our relative intelligence is not so high after all, and I am curious about what AI will unleash when it is developed by "beings" smarter than humans...

What is our purpose as human beings once AGI is reached? Isn't struggle what defines us as humans? I don't believe in a world of abundance without struggle (that's my privileged point of view, I know there is a lot of suffering in the world).

I do think we have decades (wishful thinking) before robotics and AI come to the level it needs to be at to be self sufficient.

I need to watch Wall-E again, I sure think this is where we are heading.
Umm...I don't know. Maybe ask me before posting my writing somewhere?

I think one thing that was really interesting was the example of the caterpillar, and how we never would have come up with the idea of the caterpillar folding the leaf in on itself. I think the following discussion on how we can't conflate intelligent behaviour with general intelligence was really interesting too, and how just because something exhibits intelligent behaviour, it doesnt necessarily mean that it is intelligent, as something like a mathematical function might also be considered intelligent in that sense.

The discussions on the definition of intelligence was also interesting, although i definitely thought that a few of the definitions of intelligence were a little far-fetched. For example, I think the idea that a baby isn't intelligent is weird. Even at an early stage, a baby can perceive and respond to external stimuli in ways that inanimate objects like tables can't. Like if i were to raise my fist at a baby and motion to punch it, it would know to cry or respond in a way that signifies it knows that its going to get hurt. Similarly, a caterpillar would react to external stimuli, like if it saw fire it would know to run away.

I think what I've kind of realised is that intelligence should kind of be treated as more of a spectrum rather than a strict binary behaviour, and caterpillars and babies are simply on the lower end of what it means to be 'intelligent'.

I'm not sure if I'm necessarily in the camp of believing that AI is intelligent (yet), but I think that my definition of what it means to be 'intelligent' has been swayed. Initially, I kind of defined it as an ability to respond to external stimuli, but I'm realising now that that definition is probably a bit too broad to be correct.

If just respond to stimuli was enough, then plants, basic algorithms, robots/alarm systems could technically be classified as intelligent. But this intuitively seems wrong, and so I think intelligence is more of an ability to learn and respond to external stimuli.

I think thinking of it in the sense of why it is responding also helps. If something is responding purely because of pre-defined functions/mappings (like a mathematical function or code), that's markedly different than a living creature adapting and learning. Although with this, it also begs the question as to whether an LLM, 'adapting and learning' makes it intelligent. To that, I'm not really sure what answer I have.

what really defines what intelligence is? I think one thing that really stuck out to me in class is how we all kind of had different ideas of what intelligence is, and how fallible all of our definitions of intelligence were. It also made me rethink whether some behaviour that is considered to be proof of human's intelligence is really intelligent? For example, when we discussed in class the critique of LLMs as just 'next-token-predictors', and how we don't necessarily know if that's how we as humans speak too.

To do more research into this, I'd like to look more into the neuroscience of the brain, and see if there's any research going on on how we as humans structure our sentences and the sequence of thought that happens as we are speaking/preparing to speak.

I primarily enjoyed the discussions on what we consider as intelligence in this week's discussions. I related with the discussion on topics like representations for intelligence like if early human beings hadn't invented a form of representation like alphabets, numbers, and language, then we could probably never have reached the level of knowledge we have in the modern day. There would be no way we could communicate and transfer the knowledge. Although at this point, probably some skills are passed down to us innately in our DNA like how the caterpillar protects itself by forming the protective leaf cover, but a vast majority of what we know today couldnt have been possible without communication and basically a representation for knowledge transfer.

The topic about the discussion about Einstein being a genius, because a collective group in the society thought he was a genius and propogated that further is a very unique thought. I also enjoyed the conversation about if we would consider Einstein intelligent if he was intelligent if he couldnt retain anything but could come up with new thoughts from past experiences. I believe i would definitely consider Einstien to be an intelligent creature even in that case because he can still come up with new and innovative thoughts and just record it and then someone else could actually form a ground breaking work with his chain of innovative thoughts. Apart from the intelligence aspect in terms of the solving problems, I would still consider Einstein intelligent because he still has emotional intelligence, he can feel pain, feel calmness in a good environment etc.

The two topics that stood out to me personally was about the discussions on the how intelligence requires free will and consiciousness and do we humans actually have free will? and the discussion on the fact that every action a human does can ultimately boil down to be viewed as a selfish action. 

The first discussion about free will was initially unsettling. We generally view algorithms in mathematics or models to be optimization functions for a certain objective, but viewing the human brain in a similar way, i.e., that it is merely optimizing something based on the situation at that point in time, is a very new and unacceptable view at first. It puts the whole of human intelligence and every thought and everything we do down to just being the output of a function and removes the humanness from it, is how I feel. Having never thought of human beings or the brain as just being a function that outputs certain actions based on some environmental factors is a very abstract way to think about us, but if we try and look at it in a detached way and not care about the fact that we are talking about the human brain like how we talk about machines, then to some extent it probably is right. Free will might be an illusion only in some cases, but in other cases, for example, the language switching which I mentioned in class, i.e., me speaking in different languages in different scenarios, it could be me choosing consciously as to which language and what context I want to speak in. So summarizing this point, I would think that free will can still be exercised by conscious thinking, and probably for aspects like what the brain does unconsciously, we don't have free will. Linking this to intelligence, i believe that a creature being intelligent does require a consciousness aspect, without consciousness I feel we dont reason about anything as such or have a choice or opinion about a topic and that cannot be an intelligent creature.

The second thought which I felt was very intriguing is about the fact that every human action is a selfish action. This is again something i am still trying to come to terms with. The difficulty with coming to terms with this could defintely be due to the fact that we have always associated selfish as a negative word and having to associate the actions i do to that word is probably hard for my brain which only wants me to think positively about myself(which again is selfiness) and hence could be a difficult thing to grasp. I have seen this school of thought in TV shows or social media before where examples are like , if a person donates something it is not a selfish act, but the counter argument is you donate something because you want to feel good about yourself hence you are selfish. One thought I did get in the class which could be slightly sensitive but about suicides. But if we look at why people commit them, there is almost always a reason for it like something in life was stressing them out so much that the person's brain inherently thought it is best to commit suicide which is kill itself rather than bear the pain. This is slightly counter intuitive to me, if every action we do is so selfish, this selfishness should always be able to help preserve us and not perish ourselves, unless maybe it is for an offspring or a loved one whose death we couldnt bear.

Writing the above thoughts and reminding myself about the discussions we had in class, I believe along with objectivity at some level we subconciously add an emotional layer to our thinking which makes detaching ourselves and just looking at the actions we do as simply selfish or an outcome of an optimization function. I believe this emotional layer of intelligence is what makes us human and not mere machines.

I believe that I did have some changes in thoughts after this week's discussions. Before this week, we would always think of the AI models as being trained on some large scale data and being able to give out predictions and answers. This week's discussions actually helped plant the seed of curiousity in me as to what exactly is intelligence and how can we actually say if an AI agent or in general a creature is intelligent or no.

Although some thoughts have been troubling, I slowly understand the view of looking at humans in a detached way and how at the end intelligence exhibited by humans also could merely be some function that the brain chooses to optimize. This slowly leads me to think, how do we then differentiate humans and their intelligence from the AI. It is hard to accept that what humans have developed and understood over decades and centuries is the same as what AI agents can do in the modern day but that definitely is very true. In some ways though I think how can I differentiate an AI from a human is in terms of conciousness. I dont believe the current state in which AI is, it is concious. It is probably intelligent to some extent because it has understood how to reason through and relate to information it already has, but i do not believe it is concious. AI cannot feel certain things that humans can, like feeling fresh and active when we go to a beautiful destination maybe like Switzerland. 

One other way that AI is not concious is that, some of us believe in the existence of a supreme or higher power and find peace in the fact that whatever is happening in the world is willed by the supreme power which we most commonly regard to as god. Sometimes this could be a blind faith or sometimes this could looking at historic examples and proofs like the Ram Setu between India and Sri Lanka and believing in the Ramayana but we still have a sense of faith and belief. students.name: Kyle Zhikai Thompson
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers: The stand out topic was certainly the debate regarding your theory of fundamental self interest driving all human behavior. You presented the idea that there is a subconscious optimization function underlying every decision we, as people, make in our day to day life. No matter how outwardly selfless seeming human behavior presents, or how we as people consciously think about our actions, there is a selfish reason to take that action.  The example you provided were protecting the brain from the loss of a loved one in the case of self sacrifice. From my perspective, I find this argument flawed, both because I believe it is incorrect and pointless to believe in. Many of the proposed counterarguments focused on finding loop hole situations, whereas I believe the foundation of the argument itself is shaky. Why should we be led to believe there is some mysterious optimization function subconsciously shaping conscious thought to complete its bidding. The beauty of "intelligence" is that it is capable of doing things for seemingly no reason at all. Optimized or otherwise. We have the ability to follow endless loops of logic that end in deciding to behave in a way which doesn't even make sense to our own brains. Of course there is always some way to twist it so that the optimizer is trying to optimize some component of a person's life, but at a certain point you've abstracted what the optimizer is checking for that is just becomes some vague idea of "self interest". I believe we, as intelligent, 'thinking', creatures, are categorized in such a way to do our sense of self and understand of other's sense of self. How we act after that point matters a lot less.

My primary shift is in the understanding of the word 'intelligent'. It has become clear to me now that I never had much of a sense for my personal meaning of the word before our group discussion. Beyond that, I never quite realized exactly how subjective that word really is. As with your abstraction to Einstein, most words in our brains aren't really quantifiable, merely a vague notion or shape we have molded within our brains throughout our lives. Yet, while connected, every persons abstraction of a word will be slightly different. Looking at this discussion over development of "Artificial General Intelligence", it is clear how this search for grounding the abstraction that is "intelligence" has become groping in the dark.

I've always enjoyed attempting to break down and understand animal behavior. Within the context of our group discussion last Friday, I've long struggled with attempting to avoid the anthropomorphization of animal behavior I simply don't have a complete understanding of. I think the caterpillar example is a great reflection for this. Obviously, it's easy to believe the caterpillar is thinking in such a human manner when we watch it precisely cut and fold a leaf to form a shelter. But, the methods in which the caterpillar has made the decisions which led to formation of the shelter wouldn't resemble human behavior in the slightest. It simply is following some urge it has developed through evolution. It's easy to apply this same belief when analyzing for intelligence in artificial systems. As a person who works in robotics, I think it is important to start forming some form of clear standard of benchmarks which can differentiate true understanding of logic and physical phenomena from some computationally efficient algorithm demonstrating better numbers after running it for some inane amount of time.

students.name: Longzhang Zhu
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
One concept from our class discussion that I consistently disagree with is the idea that every human action is subconsciously driven by selfish desire. For example, we talked about how a parent sacrificing their life for their child is a selfish act, simply because they would rather die than endure the pains of living without them. Or that supporting an obscure artist is merely a selfish act to claim credit for discovering them before they get popular.

The more we discussed it, the more it felt like the concept of selfishness was being shoehorned into every scenario. If you try hard enough, you can twist any action to appear selfish, regardless of the person's true motivation. Ironically, it feels selfish of us to dictate someone else's action as selfish just because there is a perspective that anything someone does is selfish.

Part of my frustration might stem from the word itself where I associate selfishness with being callous and self-interested. The notion that true selflessness is a unachievable, and that human connections amount to nothing more than a cold, transactional ledger, feels fundamentally wrong. Ultimately, if the word selfish can be stretched to describe absolutely everything, it effectively describes nothing and becomes a completely meaningless label.

One of the main things that has changed in my mind is how I view the concept of intelligence across different species, and whether true intelligence currently exists in AI. I thought about the caterpillar example, where it cleverly pulls a leaf over itself to hide from predators. (I felt pretty validated when I guessed that, if I were a caterpillar, I’d forage at night to avoid birds, which the video said was the right strategy). However, while this strategy is effective, it is still a rigid, species-level adaptation rather than individual intelligence. As Florian pointed out, countless caterpillars had to die for evolution to eventually arrive at this strategy. If you were to place that same caterpillar in a drastically different environment, like one where the leaves are too thick or brittle to bend, it would likely perish. It would take generations for the species to evolve a new solution.

Humans possess a level of intelligence that separates us from that basic programming. We don't have to wait for evolutionary mutations to survive a changing environment. As individuals, we have the unique ability to adapt to entirely new situations that we have never encountered before. While we do pass down inherited knowledge to our descendants, our true strength as a species is our capacity to piece together new survival strategies quickly.

It sounds like AI is rapidly approaching this kind of adaptable intelligence, since an AI contains vastly more information than any single human could ever possess. As long as it is provided with enough context about its outside environment, it can synthesize that massive well of knowledge to figure out creative strategies and solve problems it was never explicitly programmed for. While I don't think AI is fully there yet because getting that outside context still requires a human to prompt it, it is getting incredibly close.

I feel like whenever we discuss things like consciousness or intelligence, we always end up using some sort of animal to either confirm or counter an argument. It got me wondering about where the line actually is between biological instinct and real intelligence. I want to read more research papers on different species to see how they actually work and adapt. That way, I'll have a better understanding of the science to help support or disprove the arguments I hear in future classes.

students.name: Marija Jovic
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
One thing that stood out to me during our discussion was that we were dancing around, or perhaps rediscovering, determinism. Determinism is a philosophical movement I’m decently acquainted with, my friends and I spent an entire evening arguing free will v.s. determinism in a Willard dorm room our freshman year. That evening I sided with determinism, my analytical brain thoroughly convinced that cause and effect chains negate our ability to move as free agents through the world. I don’t agree with that anymore. However, I also do disagree that free will has anything to do with intelligence. That is what I would like to address here. 

 

It’s decently hard to argue what internally causes intelligence, as we didn’t and maybe never will come to a consensus on its definition. It might be easier, for the sake of my argument, to start with a definition of intelligence that is only defined by external signs. Imagine an alien hive mind that possesses external signs of intelligence, and is therefore intelligent. If the individuals in this hive mind are a blend of assimilated and natural-born (meaning born to the hive mind) individuals, would we naturally question the intelligence of either agent? If both the assimilated and natural-born members of the mind have their own unique ways of doing things and interacting, yet are still share a greater mind, do they not both have “choice” and a lack of it? And yet, are they not both intelligent beings, by our predetermined external definition? 

 

This is a very roundabout way of saying that if we believed in free will, or if we believed in determinism, the debate is about those philosophies. Never once did anyone in the class say something along the lines of, “If determinism is true, then we aren’t intelligent.” Humanity being intelligent is taken for granted, and so the philosophies that shape our choices have to bend to that thought, not the other way around. I think this is because we don’t have a set definition of intelligence (it changes from field to field depending on what subject we’re pursuing), but we are basing the idea of it on the fact that we have it. 

 

I liked our discussion on determinism, but I think that topic is incredibly overwhelming. Does it not strike fear in you that responsibility and choice are dead in the water? It seems to me that even philosophers struggle with this concept, so I’m not surprised it was difficult for us to counter. Mark Balaguer’s article, linked below, demonstrates that quantum physic’s probabilistic laws counter “classic determinism”, as they demonstrate that there are events in the world that “just happen.” From there he builds up new, stronger arguments for determinism, which I believe he ends up countering in his book. Frankly it is not a class on determinism, but the topic is so interesting and important to our everyday lives. I think we should all give it a thorough examination. 

 

However, what is more relevant, is that determinism/free will have nothing to do with what we view as intelligence. So we can thankfully, for now, push this monstrous philosophical argument to the side. 

 

https://thereader.mitpress.mit.edu/determinism-classical-argument-against-free-will-failure/

I think my understanding of intelligence has definitely changed following this week's discussion. My background is in cognitive science, so whenever we talked about intelligence or ideas connected to it, the implicit definition centered human intelligence. What has most come into question is the singularity of that definition of intelligence. Before this week, I was of the belief that there has to be one definition of intelligence, one way of viewing intelligence. Our conversation this week has not entirely convinced me otherwise, but it has raised some concerns. 

 

On one hand, I believe a standard definition is necessary for us to evaluate if something is or isn’t classified as part of a category. Without a standard definition of intelligence, how will we know what criteria an entity needs to fulfill to be intelligent? But also, I worry that this approach is too limiting. I’ll call back to the alien example, because it is foreign enough to be easy to use, but I think this argument can definitely apply to AI as well. Let's say we encounter an alien form of life, which is entirely foreign to us, not only in terms of appearance, but also in the nature of its very being. What if this entity has ways of existing that we have never even thought of? Forget massive spaceships and weapons of magnificent force, but a species that is and uses dark matter in a way that we simply have no method of conceiving. How do we judge their intelligence based on our criteria? How would they judge ours? 

 

Logically, there would have to be some shifts to our definition of intelligence. Our external and internal identifiers would have to move around to fit. Should intelligence have an ever-evolving definition, that concedes and adds new stipulations based on our new friends? It seems that I’ve argued myself into a corner concerning AI’s intelligence.

How could we refute determinism? This is the primary question that has arisen in my mind. I won’t lie and claim to be unbiased, determinism makes me uncomfortable, and I would like to philosophically prove that it isn’t true. Of course, if all evidence points to the contrary, I am willing to make a concession there. I think that Mark Balaguer’s article on free will was very interesting due to its usage of quantum mechanics to refute classic determinism. His book would be a good place to start. I also enjoy the usage of arguments about the brain, thought experiments, and religion to not necessarily argue that we have free will, but rather that the evidence against it isn’t strong enough (classic philosopher style). 

 

A second question that interested me is: What are the most popular definitions of intelligence in different fields of study, and do they have any common ground? It would be helpful to use currently existing knowledge as the base for our understanding and arguments. I intend to explore this question through general web searches, using perhaps encyclopedia Britannica as my starting point. It would be fun to go into this upcoming class with a well-formed definition of intelligence, and then see which newly present information shoots this definition down.

answers:
The most interesting topic to me was deciding the threshold for intelligence. First, I would like to explain what I realized during the discussion. It struck me that maybe there is no threshold, in the sense that even the simplest creatures, like a single-celled amoeba, have a certain level of intelligence. It is just the complexity that increases and makes us feel that human intelligence is different.

I would like to support my point using an analogy. Since we were also talking about whether machine intelligence is different, I realized that the basic building block of any complex architecture in AI is neurons. If I have a very simple two-neuron setup, it is a line-fitting ML model, but in a transformer, they number in the billions, and the capabilities achieved just through scaling (there are some model changes as well) are amazing. With almost the same building block, we get intelligence at the LLM level, but if you look at the simpler version, a line-fitting ML model hardly looks intelligent at all.

I believe it is the same for organisms. A single-celled amoeba has less compute and less data, so it does not appear intelligent. But if you just scale that up with huge compute (the human brain) and huge data (rich sensors like eyes, touch, etc.), the result seems like a completely different kind of intelligence. I believe they are quite similar; it is just the scaling.

Now, coming to the discussion, I found it interesting. I will first address the point that felt most on point to me: optimization. I feel there is definitely an optimization happening at different levels, which gives rise to different levels of intelligence. There are reflexes, the things you do without intent, but they are optimized for short time-domain actions and seem less intelligent. Then, as you increase the time domain, complex things like empathy, love, and emotions develop, which are important for living and communicating in a group and improving your chances of survival. In game theory, we have seen that sometimes forgiving and caring leads to long-term results (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mScpHTIi-kM). So all the points discussed, consciousness, free will, intuition, etc., are just complex evolutions of the same mechanism. It is like millions of years of optimization on data collected through our sensors and passed on generation after generation, giving rise to all these complicated-looking intelligent traits.

One point that I would like to discuss separately is learning. This is something that has confused me a bit, as I cannot explain learning through what I discussed above. Amoebas have remained the same for millions of years, but complex animals can learn. Uniquely, humans can learn in a way that is frankly very different from any other animal species, which are more or less doing the same things they have done for millions of years, while humans have gone to an entirely different level. I would say the human case is quite different, and it confuses me, as simple extrapolation does not explain it.

The thoughts that I shared in question 1, specially the part where I felt there is no threshold for intelligence, developed after the discussion in class. Some part of it came from many discussions over the past years with friends.

It has led to an uncomfortable battle inside me. I have generally been very critical of machines as not being intelligent. I would consider them as just algorithms that recognize patterns. That stance is still the same. What has changed after the discussion is how I think about human intelligence. I am starting to feel like even human intelligence is not special; it is also a simple recipe with huge compute and data. I was very critical of people comparing human brains with computers. My stand on computers has not changed, but now I am thinking less of human intelligence as something special.

There are two questions:

(i) Based on the hypothesis that I developed, that human intelligence is nothing but the scaling of single-celled organism intelligence, where does learning fit in? Amoebas are learning nothing new, while humans have learned a lot. Learning is like an update in your internal model or the weights of the model. Does it exist only in complex organisms, or is it present in simple ones as well and we just cannot realize it? So basically, I would like to go deep into learning.

(ii) Learning can be seen in other animals, but the human level is different. More or less, every animal has been doing the same thing for millions of years. What is the cause that humans are nothing like the ones from 20,000 years ago? Is the human case special, or can it be explained using extrapolation?

The following points are not part of today's discussion, but they are my concerns, and I would like to explore that through this course

(iii) Whether the Hype is real? Or if it is a bubble and we will face economic challenges like housing 2008.

(iv) I am much worried on social implication and inequality of wealth, I would like what can we as a society make sure that ensures there is equality of distribution of the produce. We are sure productivity will increase, but how will it be shared.

(v) Does it invite another major social reform like during industrial revolution, ideas of Marxism and communism developed due to exploitation of labour. Do we need or will it lead a new social reform?
