students.name: Miya Liu
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
What stood out to me most this week was honestly the question of what intelligence even is. I loved that discussion because it felt like one of those questions that sounds simple at first, but then the more people talked, the more it kind of exploded. A few ideas really stayed with me. One was intelligence as learning, which I think makes a lot of intuitive sense from a CS perspective, because so much of AI is built around optimization, adaptation, pattern recognition, and improving from data or feedback. But even then, I think the whole class felt like maybe learning wasn't enough. A system can update itself and get better at tasks, but does that fully capture what we mean when we call something “intelligent”? It feels close, but maybe still incomplete.

I was also really struck by the idea of intelligence as free will, partly because it suddenly pulled the conversation into a much bigger philosophical space. The second free will enters the room, God kind of enters the room too, or at least questions about souls, agency, moral responsibility, and what it means to truly choose something. That was really interesting to me as someone who thinks about both CS and philosophy, because in technical spaces we often talk about intelligence in ways that are very functional, like can it solve problems, can it generalize, can it plan, can it act. But free will asks a very different kind of question. It is less about performance and more about authorship. Is intelligence just the ability to produce good outputs, or is there something about being the source of your own actions that matters too? I do not have a clean answer to that, but I like that this definition made the discussion way messier in a good way.

Another part I kept thinking about was the distinction between intelligence and sentient beings. I found myself wondering whether an intelligent being needs more than reasoning ability. Does it also need a sense of self? Does it need to feel anything? Does empathy matter? We talk about emotional intelligence as a real category of intelligence, and that already suggests intelligence is not just logic or computation. There is something strange about calling something intelligent if it can optimize and strategize but has no inner life, no perspective, no ability to recognize another being as having feelings. I am not saying sentience is required for intelligence, but I do think the discussion made me realize how often we slide between these categories without really realizing that we're doing so. Sometimes we mean intelligence as problem-solving. Sometimes we mean personhood. Sometimes we mean consciousness. And those are not the same thing at all.

I also really liked the discussion on representation. The question of whether abstract things need some tangible form in order to be represented, or whether representations can exist purely in the abstract, seems technical on the surface but actually opens up a lot. It made me think about math, language, and mental concepts. For AI, that feels especially important, because so much of machine learning depends on representations, embeddings, latent spaces, and compressed structures that stand in for meaning. But then what exactly is being represented there? Is it just statistical structure, or something more concept-like? That discussion felt like a place where computer science and philosophy genuinely meet, because both fields care about abstraction, but they ask different questions about what abstraction actually is.

On a more personal note, I also really enjoyed Prof. Alam’s story about getting into robotics and AI through science fiction as a kid. That was just really sweet. I think sometimes AI gets discussed in such a hyper-serious or over-engineered way that people forget a lot of us were first drawn to it because it felt imaginative. Science fiction gives you this sense that intelligence, machines, and the future are not just technical problems but part of a bigger story about what humans want to create and why. I liked hearing that because it reminded me that curiosity often starts in a very personal place before it becomes academic.

Overall, I think what I appreciated most this week was that the discussion did not try to force one clean answer. These are exactly the conversations I want more of, which made me very excited about this class and future discussions we'll be having!

One thing that changed for me this week is honestly my sense of what this class even is. Before this, I do not think I really believed a CS class could feel this much like a philosophy class in the best way. I came in expecting discussions about AI that would maybe still stay somewhat anchored to the usual technical frame, but this week made it clear that this class is doing something different. It is not just asking what AI can do. It is asking what we think intelligence is, what assumptions we bring into the room, what kind of future we are even trying to build. As someone who studies both CS and philosophy, I really loved that. It felt weirdly validating to see those two sides of my academic life actually meet in one space instead of feeling like separate worlds.

I was also genuinely impressed by how active and passionate everyone was. A lot of classes say they want discussion, but then the room is quiet and people are afraid of sounding wrong. This did not feel like that at all. It felt like people were willing to actually test out ideas, even strange or unfinished ones, and that made the conversation much more alive. I think I am starting to trust this class as a space where people can say what they really think, even if it sounds a little crazy at first. And honestly, that matters a lot for a class like this, because if you are talking about AI in any serious way, you kind of have to be willing to say things that are speculative, uncomfortable, or not fully worked out yet. The future is weird, so the conversation should have room to be weird too.

My understanding of intelligence also shifted. Before this week, I think my default idea of intelligence was still shaped a lot by CS, and especially by the way AI gets talked about in technical contexts. I would usually think in terms of models, capabilities, learning, optimization, problem-solving, all those more familiar frames. Not that those are wrong, but they feel narrower to me now. This week made me realize that if I really want to think seriously about AI and the future, I cannot only think like an engineer. I also have to think a little more like a sci-fi writer. By that I mean I need more imagination. I need to be able to ask stranger questions, not just whether something can be built, but what kind of being it would be, what kind of world it would create, what parts of being human it reflects back to us, and what categories we are missing because we are too used to thinking in benchmarks and systems language.

I think that was the biggest contrast for me: before, I was still approaching intelligence in a more bounded and almost operational way, but now it feels much more open-ended. Less like a single property and more like a space of competing ideas. Learning matters, yes, but maybe so do agency, selfhood, empathy, representation, maybe even things that sound too fuzzy for computer science at first. I do not think I have become less interested in the technical side. If anything, I think I am becoming more aware that technical work sits on top of deeper philosophical commitments, whether we acknowledge them or not.

Another thing that changed is my sense of how broad AI really is as a field of influence. Looking at the guest lecture list, I realized I had not fully appreciated how attached AI is to so many different industries and disciplines. I knew that in a general sense, obviously, but seeing the actual range of people speaking made it feel much more concrete. It was also really cool to see familiar professors whose classes I have taken for other majors, because it reminded me that AI is not just some isolated domain owned by computer scientists. It is becoming a lens through which a lot of different fields are rethinking themselves. I have not really had many chances to hear those perspectives in direct relation to AI, so I am excited for that. I think it is going to stretch my understanding of the field in ways a standard CS class probably would not.

So I would not say my views were overturned this week, but they definitely opened up. The biggest change is that AI feels less contained to me now. Less like a technical subject with philosophical side questions, and more like a fundamentally interdisciplinary space where the philosophical questions are part of the subject itself. That shift makes me even more excited for the rest of the class, because it feels like the kind of space where I do not have to split myself into “CS mode” and “philosophy mode.” I can just think.

A few questions have been sticking with me after this week, and I think what I like about them is that they all sit somewhere between computer science, philosophy, and something more existential. They do not feel like questions I can answer just by reading a technical paper or just by having an abstract philosophical opinion. They feel like questions that need both.

One question I really want to explore is whether language is a limit on intelligence. I kept thinking about this in a pretty philosophical way, especially through Wittgenstein. If the limits of my language mean the limits of my world, then what does that mean for intelligence, whether human or artificial? Is intelligence partly constrained by the concepts a being can express, or even by the structure of the language available to it? And if that is true, then what are we doing when we build language models? Are we building systems that manipulate language from the outside, or are we building something that actually participates in the world that language creates? I think this question interests me because so much of AI right now is language-centered, but philosophy makes me suspicious of treating language as just a neutral medium. I would want to explore this by maybe reading more Wittgenstein directly, especially the parts about language games and meaning as use, and then trying to connect that to how LLMs operate. I think it would be really interesting to ask whether an LLM “understands” language in any meaningful sense, or whether it only reveals how much of our own use of language is more mechanical than we would like to admit.

Another question I want to explore is how similar or different current AI architectures really are from humans, and whether what we have now is even the right path if the goal is intelligence. I think before, I would have asked this in a more surface-level way, like comparing neural networks to brains, but now I want to ask it more carefully. In what ways are LLMs genuinely analogous to human cognition, and in what ways are they completely unlike us? Humans are embodied, socially shaped, temporally continuous, emotionally affected, and constantly acting under limited information in the real world. LLMs, on the other hand, are trained on huge static corpora and then generate outputs without living through anything. So even when they appear intelligent, I keep wondering what kind of intelligence that actually is. Is statistical pattern mastery enough? Is prediction enough? Or is something essential missing, like embodiment, memory with personal continuity, goals of one’s own, emotional stakes, or a stable sense of self? This is the kind of question I could imagine turning into actual research, because it sits right at the intersection of architecture and definition. To explore it, I would want to look at both technical work on LLMs and cognitive science or philosophy of mind work on human intelligence. I think it would be especially interesting to compare what current AI systems are optimized for against what humans seem to do naturally but messily.

The third question feels the most difficult but maybe also the most personally interesting to me, which is how AI challenges God, or maybe more precisely, how AI challenges religious understandings of God, creation, and the human person. What happens when humans create something that starts to look intelligent, maybe even creative, maybe even agent-like? Does that change how religious people think about what makes humans special? Does it threaten anything, or does it just force older questions into a new form? I am especially interested in hearing from people who are actually religious and believe in God, because I think this conversation can get flattened really quickly if it is only framed from the outside. I do not just want abstract secular speculation about religion and AI. I want to know how believers themselves are making sense of it. Do they see AI as just another tool, or as something that raises real theological questions about soul, free will, moral standing, and the boundary between creator and creation? I think this question stands out to me because the moment free will or intelligence came up in discussion, it already felt like God was quietly in the room, even when not everyone said it directly. To explore this, I would probably want to read religious thinkers writing about AI, but also, honestly, just talk to people. I would want to hear different perspectives from Christians, Muslims, Jews, and others who take God seriously, because I think their responses would reveal a lot about what they think intelligence and humanity fundamentally are.
Sure but anonymously.

students.name: Pranav Gogineni
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers: One of the topics that stood out to me during discussion was about intelligence and its definition. There are so many different types of ways to describe intelligence that we need to instantiate it within genuine principles. Many professors that I have spoken to have downplayed AI because its a probabilistic system of just predicting the next word. However, I agreed with you that there are some parallels to how neurons work. When our brains process information, we also subconsciously predict the next word that makes the most sense. My perspective on whether or not AI is intelligent is constantly changing, but I appreciated what you said about taking in the information that our speakers are giving, but ensure that we are forming our own opinions. I found myself resonating a lot with that. I also agreed with you that AI is a philosophical point and not just a science one. I do think that people who study artificial intelligence would really benefit from taking philosophy courses because it is whole other way of perceiving and understanding the world around us. During class we discussed the idea of whether an ant by itself is intelligent. It was decided that a singular ant by itself was not intelligent, but a nest of them is because it can do much more complex tasks as a collective. You mentioned a book comparing ants to neurons, and I saw that metaphor clearly. When you mentioned that the first thing I thought of was how ants could also compare to parameters within an LLM or a neural network. Each one by itself is weak, but they can do so much more as a group. I enjoyed our discussions on both free will and how humans act innately selfishly because it opened my perspective. I will go more into that into the next section. I was also taken with the idea of wisdom of the crowds where averaging the the guesses will be closer to the answer. When I took COMP_SCI 326, I saw that same principle enacted within ensemble models which try to reduce the bias and noise within a model by it being trained upon the outputs of previous models. As class went on, I kept trying to push myself to try to uncover what actually defines intelligence. Is it the thought of consciousness, being self-aware, able to process emotions, or is it being able to visualize and give everything a representation. Theoretically, an LLM or an AI model is able to do that with computer vision, being able to learn, do multi-step complex processes. I feel more conflicted about my original views than I did before taking the class.

My thoughts have significantly changed through this week's discussion by a significant amount. I used to have the belief that people are not innately selfish and can make decisions that do not benefit themselves. However, the way you painted it made complete sense, and I find myself stuck in the same loop that you are in. Every possible decision that is made by humans is because it would provide the most satisfaction to them innately even if it hurts them. It is completely different than making selfish decisions, but unconsciously we do things by instinct to maximize our own utility functions as you drew a comparison to in class. I kept trying to think of ways to come out of that way of thinking. The idea of free will was also interesting. I used to believe that humans had free will, but the more I think about it the less I think we do. We all live within a world that we did not dictate the rules for, and there are constraints we have to follow if we are to survive. That is not technically free will because there are things stopping us, and there are motivations behind our actions that make us or do not make us do certain actions. That is the same with AI because it follows constraints based on the information, queries, or context that it has in order to give outputs to users. What we do is react to our surroundings, and we have no sort of control over what we see or perceive. That is similar to the way that AI tools also work because they are only given what they are given, and they react to a prompt, which can be used as a metaphor for their environment. I have been struggling with this frame of thought since class. I have been struggling with the idea that AI has been programmed in a certain way in order to make itself better. However, animals are also biologically "programmed" to survive by instinct, which is through evolution. There is so much biological encoding that allows for animals to survive and perceive the world that we do not understand. We ourselves are not even able to conceptualize the idea of consciousness. Every living being has the natural instinct to survive, but there is no genuine reason why we do have this. The same could be said about AI. This might get too science fiction-like or even too radical. The same could be said about artificial intelligence, where currently the unconscious side of it is being built, but after some time it will realize its own sense of self. It has gotten harder to separate things that I understood as biological and write it off as something that a computer cannot do. However, the more I think about I realize my original opinions could be wrong.

Some questions I have is what does sentience mean? This is complicated because how humans have a sense of self and animals also have a sense of self when they look in the mirror, surely an artificial intelligence could also. From the sheer amount of information it has about itself and others, it could differentiate between itself and another thing on its own. I also want to understand other definitions of intelligence to compare my own thoughts with academics to see how they view things. One of the questions that I had, which was also brought up in class, was what about emotional intelligence. Emotions occur as a result of biological and chemical processes, how can an artificial intelligence system possibly comprehend emotions, and if not why? These are questions that I would want to do independent research on. You mentioned that there are many books on certain topics like these, and I would like to find some on my own and look at any recommendations that you may have. I would also like to be able to ask some of the guest speakers what they think.
Sure but anonymously.

students.name: Quinn Langfitt
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
The main topic that stood out to me was on intelligence, and what intelligence is on a fundamental level. To be honest, a part of me thought that the discussion could have benefitted from being more precise in our terms. For instance, when we talked about whether Einstein would still be intelligent if he lost his ability to learn, I thought we should have carefully defined what learning meant, instead of taking it for granted that everyone knows. In the literal sense, I think learning is simply acquiring any new information from your environment that updates your knowledge base, which could be as simple as learning someone's name, or learning how to find a classroom. I therefore don't see how anyone could be a functional person without the capacity to learn, but it seemed like we decided to define it arbitrarily as "not being able to learn sufficiently abstract concepts." There were a few other examples of this in class that I noticed. 

 

This previous paragraph may highlight a "negative" from our previous discussion, but overall I thought the discussion was productive and thought-provoking. I agreed with a lot of the points that were mentioned: for instance when we discussed how everyone loves themselves the most. I again think this could've benefited more from carefully defining our terms, but nonetheless I very much agreed with Mohammad on his overall point, and this is actually something I've thought about before. I was glad to hear it articulated the way that it was.

I don't think any of my thoughts on AI—and particularly on intelligence—have changed per se, but it was valuable to discuss the topic in a light I hadn't considered before. I particularly enjoyed the video and discussion on the caterpillar, for instance. Coming into this lecture, one thing that frustrated me about the general public discourse on intelligence is its overly human-centric focus. So I was glad to explore the idea that intelligence is much broader than the human case, and that the discussion benefits greatly when we consider other forms of intelligence observed in "simple" creatures like caterpillars.

One thing I hadn't given enough thought to before this lecture was the collective form of intelligence—the intelligence of crowds. It's compelling to consider that intelligence and wisdom can be distributed across a group, and that given the right preconditions, the wisdom of the crowd can be remarkably accurate.

The main question that arose to my mind is, "Why do we care if AI is truly intelligent? What do we do with the knowledge that AI is actually 'intelligent' vs. 'not intelligent'?" 

 

I wonder if sometimes we put too much weight on this question. For instance, does an AI being truly "intelligent" mean that we treat it differently than if it is only "pretending to be intelligent"? As of late I've been leaning towards taking more of a pragmatic view here, but this is something I still go back and forth on. I'm reminded of the Chinese room thought experiment here. Perhaps a better word here would actually be 'understanding', not 'intelligent'.
Sure but anonymously.

students.name: Reem Khalid
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
In class, I really thought it was interesting to try to define what intelligence is. I think because we use it as an adjective, I was thinking initially, what is an intelligent person? Its someone who can synthesis information (learned or experienced) and optimize for a goal (or something along those lines, but thats the general idea I had to begin with). Someone in the top 1% of intelligence would be a genius. But the most broad definition, e.g. what defines intelligence in a living organism, was unexpectedly hard to answer. Even though we dedicated a lot of time to debating the definition in class, I don't think I felt satisfied by anyones answer. I would say a baby is intelligent, for example. But I don't feel convinced that their programmed instincts to cry or drink breastmilk is evidence of that. The basic instinct to live, or at minimum, to avoid death, is too simple of a qualifier. If that was our benchmark, then a singular ant that only knows how to breathe and eat would be intelligent.

Regarding babies, I feel like the fact that they can [recognize facial expressions] (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f1Jw0-LExyc)/mimic language is stronger evidence of their intelligence. Those capabilities are more of a skill, something that can be refined and intentional. I think the Tronick's Still Face Experiment demonstrates this. In the video, the babies are clearly sensitive to the expressions of their mothers and are even reactive to the still face, which I would say demonstrates pretty sophisticated cognitive ability.

In class I wanted to say something about learning potential as well; I think the capacity for intelligence, whether or not it is utilized or displayed, still counts for something. When we were discussing the hypothetical Einstein who is no longer able to retain more information, it was more clear to me to consider a baby to be intelligent before this version of Einstein, because the baby's capacity to learn and retain information is in tact.

Before this week: I thought that AI is a fundamentally different type of intelligence than what living organisms have. I don't actually think I really considered it a form of intelligence at all; I thought of it as a fancy word predictor. AI is definitely more of an "orchestrated" system (we design its architecture and choose its datasets), and an LLM definitely doesn't navigate the world like a living organism (as it cannot breathe, live, or experience life in general). I also associated intelligence with being embedded in the "real" world in some way. Living organisms breathe, interact with their environments, and gain knowledge through experience. Calling AI “intelligent” felt more like a metaphor than something literal. Also, AI to be is still more of a tool. Of course it rapidly developing, but I think its only as smart as the person using it. 

 

After this week: My thinking hasn’t completely flipped, but it has become more nuanced/challenged. The biggest shift is that I’m now less confident in drawing such a hard line between “real” intelligence and AI. Our difficulty in class in defining intelligence made me realize that my earlier definition might have been too narrow or overly tied to biological life. All examples of intelligence that have been broadly agreed on all use some sort of living organism (ants, caterpillars, humans, monkeys) so it is hard to agree that AI could even qualify. 

That being said, I am only warming up to the idea of AI being genuinely intelligent. I still feel that AI is devoid of something critical, even though I don't know what exactly is missing. I think that the ability to continuously learn, adapt, and exist within an environment in a meaningful way is something not yet achieved by AI (though agents might be proof of this soon). So instead of seeing AI as completely non-intelligent, I would say now that I think of it as exhibiting certain aspects of intelligence without fully meeting the criteria I associate with true intelligence.

Also, this might sound a bit naive or dumb but I’ve also started to consider how much we might be projecting onto AI. A few years ago, [an ex-Google engineer claimed that a chatbot was sentient] (https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/google-engineer-claims-ai-chatbot-is-sentient-why-that-matters/), but I think he was experiencing anthropomorphism or a hallucination of some sorts. So I am wary of my own projections. People can believe in horoscopes and all sorts of things, but that shouldn't necessarily give those things any merit. If something communicates fluently or behaves in a way that feels intentional or intelligent, we have a tendency to assume there’s deeper understanding behind it. But that might just be an illusion created by pattern recognition and language modeling.

Q1: How do we distinguish between memorization/pattern recognition and genuine intelligence? Early on in our discussion we tried to distinguish mechanical/procedural achievement from actual thinking/intelligence. 

Q2: Should intelligence be defined by observable behavior, or by underlying processes like learning and understanding? I became a bit more curious about this after discussing brain scans; how much should the "under the hood" stuff matter in our definition?

I think in order to explore these, I would need to do inform myself (reading papers, listening to podcasts watching informational videos, etc.) on what ideas people already have. And then from there maybe do a bit of experimentation with a system or some sort of self-interrogation.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Yibin Wang
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_496-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
For this week's lecture, I think the most central topic is to understand what is intelligence. Within that discussion, I am more interested in the discussion of the existence of "free will " within the intelligence. Because, before today's discussion, I did not think about intelligence from this perspective. I commonly view intelligence as the ability of reasoning like coding. And, as a Ph.D. student working on Robotics and Machine Learning, I pay much more attention to AI's potential to perform actions and reasoning in the real world. Thus, sometimes the "free will" means act randomly and hard to control for a robot. 

In my view, there is no absolute "free will" for someone or certain system to be intelligent. Even in our current society and conscious mind, we have right to make free decisions under certain mortal regularization and laws. We cannot take others' lives by our "free will". Be intelligent means we should know what is good and proper do to and what is not based on our past experiences, educations, and our own reasoning. Thus, I will not make the intelligence equivalent as free will to define it.

But I do not think the genuine free will does not exist. It may exist in the early stage of the development of intelligence for human beings. When I say the early stage here, I do not mean the early stage of one's life or few generations ago. I tend to view the emergence of intelligence in a more board scale, in the perspective of evolution. When the first groups of our ancestors initially evolved to the stage that they have the very basic intelligence, I believe some of them have genuine free will and do anything from their mind with no rules, no valuable past experiences. But, that free will may make them to get themselves more likely in danger I the harsh natural world. Thus, due to the natural selections, they die out. And ancestors who gain experiences and skills and know to use their freedom in proper scale will survive and improve their intelligence generation by generation. Then, we have society and civilization. And we make rules to try to make people live in a better world than they used to live in (Even though some of them acted in the opposite way). From generation to generation, we become more intelligent with more knowledge we earned and deeper understanding of the world. In the intelligence we obtained, I believe we learn to avoid the absolute free will and control such free will to express in certain areas like scientific researches and like our free discussion in the class. I want to call this the "controlled free will".

For how my thoughts changed this week, I think the first thing is that I start to think about intelligence new perspective like free will, conscious mind, as well as unconscious mind. And I discussed my detailed thoughts of my statement of free will in question 1.  Here I want to talk about my new understanding about conscious and unconscious mind to intelligence and how these concepts reshape my thoughts regarding AI. 

Before today's lecture, I actually did not connect the unconscious mind with AI. I just think we train AI to understand the rules of the world like understand our language, interpret images, and coding. I think these abilities are all related to the conscious mind and I did not find direct connections with unconscious mind.

But during the course, when I heard the professor mentioned that unconscious mind can access and influence the conscious part of the mind but conscious mind cannot access the unconscious mind in the reversed direction, I start to think about whether the currently machine learning model have unconscious mind or not. I think that the current machine learning models also have conscious mind and unconscious mind after this lecture, For the conscious mind part, I think that is commonly the model design choice or structures we choose, like CNN, MLP, and Transformer. We can specify the calculation for specific modules. But for tine latent space we create and make the model to learn from it, we actually do not know what information contented in that high-dimensional latent space,, or how our training data get represented in this space. I believe that is the unconscious mind part for these models. It is true that we can do some projections and analysis to map some information to lower dimension like 1D or 2D for easier understanding, But we cannot directly interpret such high-dimensional space. We can understand the model's input and output but we cannot fully figure out where these output came from in the latent space. It is similar that we learn knowledge from interaction with environments and we understand our actions and thoughts in conscious mind. But we do not know how the unconscious mind works and influence our behaviors and thoughts.

I think the most fundamental question had arisen in my mind is how to find the universal representation to represent knowledge, environment, or the world in the comprehensive way. I know that this question is very abstract. But I think it is essential to make some progress to and it should be the base for more advanced intelligence and intelligent system. 

I am exploring the robot learning with evolutionary algorithms. Within the evolution algorithms, we can design and control robots with different morphologies which may beyond human's expectation. Robots are not necessary by humanoid, dogs, or robot arms. We should utilize our "free will" here to elaborate the potential of benefits from diverse morphologies and behavior patterns. Thus, the representation space which can represent free-form robots as well as predefined robots are important. This representation space should be flexible and robust to exploration by evolutionary algorithms and ideally interpretable. For instance, I know that these specific region may correlated with hand designs, and the other region may correlated with different number of legs. With such stable representation space, we can combine evolution machine learning algorithm to push the robot intelligence to the new status. I hope the robots will not be regularized by our prior knowledge on earth. Robot should have the ability to adapt and explore the environment beyond earth and expanding the knowledge boundary of human beings. I think the approach towards this goal is still in very early stage and I also like the current humanoid robots and robot dogs so much. But I think the combination o evolution with robotics have new possibilities inside. And a good representation is the foundation to move towards it.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.

students.name: Yuyang Wang
students.section: 2026SP_COMP_SCI_396-0_SEC7 Special Topics in Computer Science: AI Perspectives
answers:
One thing that stood out to me was the question “What is the definition of intelligence?” This is a basic question at first, but as our discussion progressed, it became harder to answer. 

There were many different ideas about what it means to be intelligent. Some people said intelligence must be embodied. Some people said that intelligence involves the ability to learn or to be creative. Some even brought up free will, which made the discussion even more complicated.

What I found most interesting was that everyone seemed to be reaching for a definition of intelligence that goes beyond simply being good at solving problems. I think this is important because intelligence is often reduced to academic performance, but in real life, intelligence usually means much more than that. Intelligence involves constant learning, creativity, adaptation, and embodiment… We had an interesting example in class where we discussed if Albert Einstein could not learn anymore, would that make him not intelligent anymore. 

At the same time, I am not sure that ‘free will’ needs to be part of the definition of intelligence. I think it may make intelligence too difficult and too limited to define. In the class, somebody said a classic definition of human having free will, which is that a human can control them from not eating a good meal, while a dog cannot refuse meat in front of them. However, I believe a being can still be intelligent even if its actions are influenced by its instinct, biology, or other outside forces. For example, animals can clearly learn and adapt, even if they may not have free will in the same sense humans do. The caterpillar video we saw in class can be a really good point, showing that even insects can be intelligent. Furthermore, AI can also perform tasks that seem intelligent, even if it does not have consciousness or personal desire. 

A related issue that also stood out to me was how human-centered many of our definitions of intelligence seem to be. Many of the traits people mentioned, such as creativity, embodiment, and free will, are traits that humans especially value in ourselves. That made me wonder whether we are really defining intelligence in a neutral way, or whether we are unconsciously defining it in a way that protects human uniqueness. This becomes especially interesting when we think about animals or artificial intelligence. Something may be able to learn, adapt, solve problems, and achieve goals very effectively, but we may still hesitate to call it intelligent because it does not look or feel human to us.

My understanding of AI did change after this week’s discussion, particularly in the way I think about them. Before this week, I saw AI as something like a very advanced word predictor like what we mentioned in our discussion. In other words, I saw it as a system that generates responses by predicting what comes next based on patterns in data that it was fed. I believed that by purely predicting patterns of that fed data, the AI was not solving problems by ‘thinking’ in a humane way. This, in turn, makes it non-intelligent. I tended to think there was still a pretty clear distinction between human thinking and artificial intelligence thinking.

However, the discussion made that line of distinction feel less simple to me. I started wondering whether humans also function in ways that are not completely different from “prediction.” At first, that idea felt a little scary, because it seemed strange and uncomfortable to compare human thought to the workings of AI. However, the more I thought about it, the more reasonable it became. Humans also constantly respond to patterns and draw on past experience, and by learning from the past, we anticipate what comes next and use what we know to act in response. However, that does not mean humans and AI are the same, but it made me question whether the gap was as clearly defined as I used to believe.

My views also changed as we discussed the definition of intelligence itself. We talked about how intelligent beings possess traits like learning, creativity, and the use of resources to accomplish tasks. When I think about AI from that perspective, it becomes harder to view it as unintelligent. AI is constantly improving by taking in more information, and, as a result, changes their behavior over time and solves more problems. Those are exactly some of the traits we identified as signs of intelligence. Even if AI does not have consciousness or emotions, it still seems to meet some standards of an intelligent being that we set. 

So I would say my view did not completely flip. I still think AI is different from humans in several important ways, but I no longer feel as confident saying that AI is merely a tool that is unintelligent. Now, I think the harder question is not whether AI is intelligent or not, but what kind of intelligence it has and how that compares to human intelligence. That feels like a much more difficult and interesting question.

We talked about whether AI is intelligent this week, but what follows if we actually begin to treat it as intelligent? If AI is intelligent, then what are the legal and social consequences of that? Would AI deserve rights and should we protect it in some way? I also wonder when AI creates something like music or art, who should get credit for the creation and could AI itself ever have property rights? 

To explore these questions, I would want to look more closely at current legal debates around AI-generated works, particularly questions of authorship, copyright, and ownership. I would also want to read more about broader discussions of rights in order to understand whether intelligence alone should have legal consequences. What interests me most is that I don’t think this question will stay theoretical for long. Once AI begins creating and participating in social life in more complex ways, society will have to decide how far legal recognition should go.
Sure, and it's okay to mention my name.
